sector: Industrials — Mechanical/Electrical Construction & Building Services (data-center contractor)
bull_thesis:
Comfort Systems is one of the purest, highest-quality contractor plays on the physical AI build-out. As of the first quarter of 2026, advanced-technology and data-center work accounts for 56% of revenue, and the broader industrial category is 75%, with electrical revenue up 88% on an organic basis and mechanical up 47%. The company sits squarely on the cooling and electrical-distribution bottleneck that every dollar of hyperscaler capital spending must pass through.

Execution has been exceptional. First-quarter 2026 revenue came in at $2.87 billion, up 56% year over year (51% on a same-store basis), with a record gross margin of 26.3% (roughly 25.2% excluding a $43 million project close-out), an operating margin of 17.0%, and diluted earnings per share (profit divided by the number of shares outstanding) of $10.51, up 121%. Trailing twelve-month revenue is $10.14 billion, up 38%, and trailing twelve-month earnings per share are approximately $34.65, up 108%.

Returns are best in class. Return on invested capital (ROIC, the profit generated for every dollar the business has deployed) is roughly 62%, and return on equity (ROE) is roughly 53%, on a structurally capital-light, negative-working-capital business model — meaning the company collects cash from customers before it pays its suppliers, so growth is self-funding. The balance sheet holds approximately $1.05 billion in cash against roughly $39 million in debt, for net cash of about $1.0 billion (approximately $29 per share), and free cash flow (cash left after maintaining the business) was $632 million over the nine months ended 2025, with $242 million in the first quarter of 2026 alone.

The order book stands at a record $12.45 billion, up roughly 80% year over year and $500 million ahead of last quarter, with management describing technology bookings as 'especially strong,' giving the company multi-quarter revenue visibility. The modular prefabrication business — now 17% of revenue and on track to reach 4 million square feet of capacity by end of 2026, a roughly 30% expansion — is a genuine and growing competitive advantage: it lets Comfort Systems sell speed of build to hyperscalers at a time when speed is the scarcest commodity in the AI race.

Management's tone is the most confident in company history, describing demand as 'insatiable' with 'pipelines full coast to coast' and 'plenty more work we could take if we could possibly do it.' Guidance credibility is high — the company's full-year 2026 same-store growth target of mid-to-high 20% against tough comparables is characteristically conservative. UBS raised its price target to $2,125 on 8 June 2026, explicitly citing the data-center-led backlog. The thesis holds as long as hyperscaler capital spending stays durable for four to eight or more quarters and Comfort Systems maintains its pricing and labor edge in a supply-constrained market.
bear_thesis:
You are paying a secular-growth multiple — 56.8 times trailing earnings per share, 44.5 times forward earnings, 39.4 times enterprise value divided by earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortisation (EV/EBITDA), 50 times price to free cash flow, and 6.8 times price to sales — for peak-cycle margins on peak-cycle volume in a single end market, after the stock ran from a 52-week low near $490 to roughly $1,967, approximately a fourfold gain in a year. Almost all of the AI-capital-spending optionality is already in the price; this is momentum extended after a re-rating, not a fear-print.

56% of revenue now sits in data centers, so the entire thesis rides the same single shared macro driver — the durability of AI-related capital spending — as the rest of the scale-out basket. That is precisely the concentration the current market regime says to avoid.

The competitive moat is narrow. Contracting is a competitive, low-switching, re-bid-project-by-project business. Today's record 26.3% gross margin reflects a supply-constrained market where contractors hold transient pricing power, not structural lock-in. When data-center bookings normalise or labour costs catch up, incremental margins will compress and ROIC will mean-revert away from cyclically inflated peaks — and the market is capitalising those peaks as permanent.

Management itself names labour as the binding constraint ('plenty more work we could take if we could'), so the upside surprises that drove the re-rating are mechanically harder to repeat. Meanwhile, the company is committing fixed modular factory capacity, which creates operating-deleverage risk if the demand curve rolls.

The order book is a coincident or lagging indicator of confidence that historically peaks with the cycle. The load-bearing catalyst — the data-center backlog and margin re-rating — has largely already fired. A hyperscaler capital-spending slowdown, the basket's shared tripwire, would de-rate a 45-to-57 times multiple sharply; there is essentially no valuation margin of safety to cushion it.
sop_bull: Approximately $2,250 to $2,400 per share. The business operates as one integrated mechanical and electrical contracting company rather than as separable divisions, so this is a whole-company intrinsic-value estimate by metric rather than a true sum-of-the-parts (valuing each business line separately). A full-year 2026 run-rate earnings-per-share estimate of $42 to $45 — annualising the first-quarter $10.51 figure with the guided second-half deceleration — at a 'durable grower' multiple of roughly 50 times earnings produces an equity value of approximately $2,100 to $2,250; adding net cash of roughly $29 per share gives roughly $2,130 to $2,280. A stretched bull scenario capitalises a roughly $50 forward earnings-per-share figure (assuming the modular business reaches its full 4-million-square-foot capacity and the technology mix continues to rise) at 47 to 48 times earnings, which produces approximately $2,350, plus net cash, for a total near $2,380.
sop_bear: Approximately $1,050 to $1,250 per share, based on through-cycle normalisation. A through-cycle earnings-per-share estimate of $28 to $32 — haircut for margins reverting to roughly 20 to 21% gross and 12 to 13% operating as supply-constraint pricing fades and labour catches up, on still-elevated but post-peak volume — at a 'narrow-moat cyclical contractor' multiple of 32 to 36 times (in line with peers such as EMCOR and API Group, rather than a secular-software multiple) produces roughly $950 to $1,100; adding net cash of roughly $29 per share gives approximately $980 to $1,130. In a harder bear scenario where AI-capital-spending digestion causes backlog to burn down without replacement, gross margin reverts to the low 20s, and EV/EBITDA compresses to a peer-normal 18 to 22 times on roughly $1.3 to $1.5 billion in normalised EBITDA, the implied equity value drops to roughly $780 to $960 per share.
sop_breakdown: Reconciled whole-company intrinsic value. The base-case earnings-per-share estimate of $34 to $38 sits above the through-cycle floor but below the first-quarter-annualised peak — it credits durable but decelerating data-center demand and the modular capacity ramp, but does not treat a 26.3% gross margin as a permanent plateau. Applying a multiple of 40 to 42 times earnings — a premium contractor multiple that respects elite ROIC, the net-cash balance sheet, and modular optionality, but sits well below the current 44.5 to 56.8 times, because 56% single-end-market concentration and peak margins do not merit a secular-software re-rating — produces roughly $1,430 to $1,600 in equity value; adding net cash of roughly $29 per share gives approximately $1,460 to $1,630. The midpoint is roughly $1,550. The gap between roughly $1,967 (current price) and roughly $1,550 represents the AI-capital-spending optionality that is already priced in.
risk_reward: Unfavourable at roughly $1,967. Upside to the bull fair-value estimate of roughly $2,370 is approximately 21%. Downside to the base fair-value estimate of roughly $1,550 is approximately 21%. Downside to the bear estimate of roughly $1,150 is approximately 42%, and downside to a capital-spending air-pocket scenario of roughly $900 is approximately 54%. The reward-to-risk ratio is roughly 1 to 2 against entry at current levels — roughly symmetric upside but a much fatter, more probable downside tail, because the load-bearing catalyst has largely already fired and the multiple prices peak margins as permanent.
catalyst_status: FIRED (with a SOFT_EXTENDED tail). The load-bearing catalyst — the data-center backlog inflection + record-margin re-rate — has substantially happened: backlog already printed $12.45B (+74% YoY), Q1-2026 EPS +121%, GM a record 26.3%, the stock ~4x off its 52-week low to ~$1,967, the long-running buyback completed, dividend raised to $0.80, and UBS lifted its PT to $2,125 (Jun-8-2026). The re-rate is spent. The residual is a SOFT_EXTENDED continuation bet (each future quarter must re-print 'insatiable' demand to hold the multiple) hinging on the same shared AI-capex factor as the whole basket — not an asymmetric, dated, idiosyncratic catalyst. Next hard date: Q2-2026 print (~late-July 2026), which is a continuation/confirmation event, not a re-rating trigger.
peer_comps_note:
The true peer group is the mechanical and electrical specialty-construction contractors with meaningful data-centre and electrical build-out exposure: EMCOR (EME), Quanta Services (PWR), MasTec (MTZ), and API Group (APG). Live multiples as of June 2026: Comfort Systems trades at roughly 56.8 times trailing earnings, 44.5 times forward earnings, 39.4 times EV/EBITDA, and 6.8 times price to sales, with a trailing twelve-month ROIC of roughly 62%.

EMCOR (the closest direct comparable at roughly $41.6 billion market cap, first-quarter 2026 revenue $4.63 billion up 19.7%) trades at roughly 30.4 times trailing earnings and 26 to 32 times forward earnings — materially cheaper at roughly half Comfort Systems' multiple on slower but still-solid growth. Quanta Services (roughly $651 per share; record order book $48.5 billion; estimated to be roughly 65% above a widely used fair-value estimate) trades at roughly 95 times trailing earnings, making it even more expensive than Comfort Systems but with more grid and utility diversification and less pure data-centre concentration. MasTec at roughly $358.50 per share trades at roughly 63.3 times trailing earnings and 40.1 times forward earnings, with full-year earnings-per-share guidance up roughly 33% — comparably rich.

Within this cohort, Comfort Systems ranks first on revenue growth (up 56% total, 51% same-store — fastest in the group), first on margins and capital returns (26.3% gross margin, roughly 62% ROIC — best in the group), and first or tied for first on AI-capital-spending exposure (56% of revenue from data centres — the most concentrated name). On valuation it is the second-most-expensive, below Quanta's roughly 95 times trailing earnings but well above EMCOR's roughly 30 times and roughly in line with MasTec.

The conclusion: Comfort Systems is the highest-quality, fastest-growing, and most data-centre-concentrated name in the peer group — and it is priced accordingly. EMCOR offers the cleanest valuation cushion in the cohort and shares the same tailwind. Quanta is the most stretched. Comfort Systems' premium to EMCOR is partly deserved given superior margins, returns, and modular intellectual property, but trading at roughly twice EMCOR's earnings multiple is difficult to justify when both companies benefit from the same demand tailwind.
role_in_scaleout:
Comfort Systems is a frontline mechanical and electrical build-out contractor — it physically installs the cooling systems (HVAC and chilled-water loops) and electrical-distribution infrastructure (power rooms and switchgear) inside data centres, and through its modular prefabrication arm (17% of revenue, on track to reach 4 million square feet of annual capacity) it sells speed of build, the scarcest input in the AI race. The work is mission-critical to any data-centre scale-out: no data centre powers or cools without these systems.

Demand looks durable for several years given the booked $12.45 billion order book and the runway in hyperscaler capital budgets. However, the revenue model is cyclical and capital-spending-derivative — it is not a recurring-revenue or lock-in business — and the 56% single-end-market concentration ties the company's terminal demand entirely to the durability of AI-related capital spending.
consensus_delta:
There is no favourable 'where we disagree with the market' opportunity here — the street is already looking the right way and has paid for it. Consensus has fully embraced the AI data-center thesis: 56.8 times trailing earnings, 39.4 times EV/EBITDA, a roughly fourfold run off the 52-week low, and UBS raising its price target to $2,125 on 8 June 2026 explicitly citing the data-center backlog.

If there is a mispricing, it is on the other side: the street is implicitly treating the record 26.3% gross margin and 56% data-center revenue mix as a permanent plateau, when the company's own chief financial officer backed out $43 million of project-timing favourability (which puts the true underlying gross margin closer to 25.2%) and management names labour as the growth ceiling.

The genuinely shaky market assumption is that this margin and growth level is structural rather than peak-cycle pricing in a supply-constrained market. This is a saturated narrative, not a contrarian one. Even within the contractor peer group, EMCOR trades at roughly half Comfort Systems' multiple on the same tailwind — Comfort Systems is not even the mispriced-cheap way to own the theme.
valley_of_death:
There is no balance-sheet danger zone here — net cash is roughly $1.0 billion, the company is strongly free-cash-flow positive ($242 million in the first quarter of 2026 alone), there is no meaningful debt maturity wall, and the dividend is covered many times over. The risky window is one of valuation and expectations, not solvency.

Over the next three to nine months, the risks are: first, second-quarter and third-quarter 2026 results must lap same-store growth of 51% from a year ago — any deceleration or margin step-down from the 26.3% peak (which management has half-guided by flagging tougher second-half comparables and backing out the $43 million project item) will be punished hard at 45 to 57 times earnings; second, any signal from a hyperscaler of capital-spending restraint — the shared tripwire across the entire scale-out basket — would de-rate the whole cohort, including Comfort Systems; third, labour-cost catch-up compressing incremental margins. None of these is a dated binary event; all are ways a peak multiple can crack before any new positive catalyst arrives.
positioning_washout:
There is no yield-cut or dividend-cut forced-selling dynamic here — the 0.16% dividend yield is immaterial and the shareholder base is growth and momentum oriented, not income driven. The relevant mechanical risk runs in the opposite direction: Comfort Systems has been a momentum and AI-infrastructure darling (roughly fourfold gain in a year, repeated all-time highs near $2,074), so the shareholder base is performance-chasing and crowded into the AI-capital-spending trade alongside peers such as Quanta, MasTec, and Vertiv.

In an AI-capital-spending scare or a growth-to-value rotation, that holder base exits quickly and together with the rest of the cohort — a forced-selling unwind risk, not a forced-selling opportunity to buy into. There is no capitulation flush to take advantage of here; the stock has not flushed, it has melted up.
forcing_function: There is no positive catalyst that forces a re-rating upward from here — that re-rating has already happened. The relevant hard dates are confirmation or de-rating risks. The second-quarter 2026 earnings release, expected in late July 2026, is the nearest checkpoint: same-store growth must hold in the guided mid-to-high 20% range against a 51% comparable, and gross margin must stay near peak. The broader hyperscaler capital-spending print cycle — the shared basket tripwire — is the other key date cadence. The event that would shatter the bull consensus is a single hyperscaler signalling capital-spending digestion, or a Comfort Systems margin step-down; either forces multiple compression on a name with no valuation cushion.
moderator_conclusion:
Comfort Systems is an exceptional business at an unforgiving price, and the catalyst is spent. The fundamental quality is genuinely 5 out of 5 — ROIC of roughly 62%, net cash of roughly $1.0 billion, a record gross margin of 26.3%, revenue up 56%, a $12.45 billion order book, and high-credibility management that voluntarily discounts its own margin figure. Its role in the scale-out as the cooling and electrical-distribution contractor with scarce modular speed-of-build capacity is mission-critical, and demand looks durable for several years.

However, applying the current regime — which directs the portfolio to enforce resolution-driver independence and penalise names whose thesis rides a single shared AI-capital-spending factor — the conclusion is clear. The load-bearing catalyst has fired: the order book has printed at $12.45 billion up 80%, the quarterly earnings-per-share figure is up 121%, the stock has run roughly fourfold, the buyback is complete, and a major analyst has raised its price target to $2,125 chasing the tape. 56% of revenue depends on the same AI-capital-spending factor as the whole basket — a book-independence problem, not an orthogonal driver — and the market is capitalising peak-cycle margins on peak-cycle volume at 44.5 to 56.8 times earnings and 39.4 times EV/EBITDA, with no margin of safety. The base sum-of-the-parts fair value is roughly $1,550 versus a current price of roughly $1,967, implying roughly 21% downside to fair value and 42 to 54% downside to the cyclical or air-pocket cases. Reward-to-risk is approximately 1 to 2.

Within the contractor cohort, EMCOR offers the same tailwind at roughly half the multiple, so Comfort Systems is not even the inexpensive way to own the theme. This is a quality-trap-at-a-price, an already-ran name, not an asymmetric entry point. The verdict is 'pass for now,' conviction 2. If the AI-capital-spending tape flushes and Comfort Systems de-rates toward the low-30s on EV/EBITDA — implying a price in the $1,200 to $1,300 range — it becomes a high-conviction 'wait for the wash-out' or 'buy now' on quality. Not here.

Pure valuation conviction is also 2: stripping out the catalyst and regime overlay entirely and judging the stock against the roughly $1,550 sum-of-the-parts fair value plus the fundamental quality alone, the stock still trades roughly 25% above intrinsic value on capitalised peak margins. The narrow moat (which is cyclically widening) and the absence of any secular erosion threat — Comfort Systems is a beneficiary of electrification and the AI build-out, not a victim — prevent the valuation conviction from being capped lower, but cannot rescue a name trading well through fair value.
interrogator_dossier:
# FIX — Comfort Systems USA — Forensic Dossier

Prepared by the interrogator, drawing on live evidence as of 22 June 2026. Bundled transcripts were not supplied; this dossier is built from the live first-quarter 2026 earnings release and call coverage (reported 23/24 April 2026), the full-year 2025 results, the first-quarter 2026 10-Q filing, and current market data. Where figures are inferred from published numbers rather than directly quoted, that is noted.

## 1. Narrative Arc

Comfort Systems is a roll-up of mechanical (HVAC, plumbing, piping) and electrical contractors that for two decades told a steady, unglamorous story: low-to-double-digit organic growth, bolt-on acquisitions, modest mid-single-digit margins, disciplined free cash flow, and a slowly rising dividend. That is not the story the market is pricing today.

The narrative has been sharply recentred on one driver: the data-centre and 'advanced technology' build-out. As of the first quarter of 2026, advanced-technology work (data centres) is 56% of revenue and the broader industrial bucket is 75%. A company that a few years ago was predominantly commercial and institutional construction is now, in its own framing, an AI-infrastructure contractor that also does HVAC. The modular prefabrication business has gone from a side project to 17% of revenue, with capacity guided to 4 million square feet by end of 2026, explicitly to feed hyperscaler speed-of-build demand.

The shift in what drives the business is the key arc: institutional and commercial work is now the ballast, and the offensive narrative is entirely 'insatiable' technology demand, 'coast to coast,' with the binding constraint reframed from winning contracts to finding labour ('plenty more work we could take if we could possibly do it'). That is a high-class problem and a genuine demand signal — but also a tell that the cycle is at or near peak intensity.

## 2. Claims versus Financials

- 'Record revenue, record order book': Revenue $2.87 billion, up 56% year over year; same-store up 51%; order book $12.45 billion versus $6.89 billion a year earlier, up roughly 81%. Verified — real volume growth, not a revenue-mix illusion.
- 'Margins strong and largely sustainable': Gross margin 26.3% versus 22.0% a year ago; operating margin 17.0% versus roughly 11.4%. The chief financial officer voluntarily backed out $43 million of late-stage project favourability, implying an underlying gross margin closer to 25.2%. Largely verified, lightly flattered; the willingness to self-deflate is a positive integrity signal.
- 'Demand durable, pipelines full': Order book up roughly 81% year over year, up $500 million sequentially, with technology bookings described as 'especially strong.' Verified by the order-book figure, though order-book durability is precisely what the bear case questions.
- 'Returns exceptional': Return on equity roughly 53%, ROIC roughly 62% for full-year 2025 — capital-light, negative-working-capital contractor economics turbocharged by high volume. Structurally sound but cyclically inflated.
- 'Fortress balance sheet': Roughly $1.05 billion in cash versus roughly $39 million in debt (net cash roughly $1.0 billion, approximately $29 per share); free cash flow $632 million over the nine months ended 2025, $242 million in the first quarter of 2026. Verified, self-funding, no leverage risk.
- 'Earnings per share doubled': First-quarter diluted earnings per share $10.51, up 121%; trailing twelve-month figure roughly $34.65, up 108%. Verified.

Where the story goes beyond what the financials prove: the implicit claim embedded in a 56-to-44 times earnings multiple — that this growth and margin level is a new permanent plateau — is not demonstrated. The financials show peak-cycle margins on peak-cycle volume; nothing in the disclosures proves those levels hold once bookings normalise.

## 3. Tone and Confidence Trajectory

Rising, and emphatically so. Language such as 'insatiable,' 'persistent demand,' 'pipelines still very full, very strong, coast to coast,' and 'plenty more work we could take' is the most confident in the company's history. The tone has shifted from a conservative Texas-contractor register to leaning into a secular story. The single defensive note is honest and self-imposed: the chief financial officer backing out the $43 million of project close-outs and repeatedly naming labour — not demand — as the growth ceiling.

## 4. Guidance Credibility

High. The company has a long record of beating targets and setting conservative guidance. The full-year 2026 same-store growth guide of 'mid-to-high 20%' into tougher second-half comparables is characteristically cautious — it implies a meaningful deceleration from the 51% first-quarter pace. The margin guidance ('elevated recent-quarter levels') is the riskiest commitment and is hedged by the $43 million disclosure. Overall reliability: high.

## 5. Analyst Pressure Points

The recurring unresolved question on earnings calls is margin durability and order-book conversion timing: how much of the 26.3% gross margin is structural versus reflecting a supply-constrained market where contractors hold temporary pricing power, and what happens when labour costs catch up and bookings cool? The second recurring theme is hyperscaler and customer-concentration cyclicality — the same single AI-capital-spending factor that drives the entire scale-out basket. Management's response ('the order book is real, the pipeline is full') is accurate for today and silent on what happens on the far side of the cycle. Labour constraints are acknowledged openly rather than deflected.

## 6. Positive and Negative Signals

Positive: net cash of roughly $1.0 billion, self-funding the boom; ROIC roughly 62% and return on equity roughly 53%; the chief financial officer voluntarily deflated the margin figure by $43 million (a high-integrity signal); order book of $12.45 billion up roughly 81% provides visibility; dividend raised to $0.80, covered many times by free cash flow.

Negative: 56% of revenue is data centres — single-end-market AI-capital-spending concentration; peak-cycle margins are capitalised into a 44 to 56 times earnings multiple (mean reversion in either volume or margin de-rates sharply); the stock has run roughly fourfold off the 52-week low to roughly $1,967 (optionality priced in, not a fear print); labour is the binding constraint, making further upside surprises mechanically harder to deliver; the order book is a coincident and lagging comfort indicator that peaks with the cycle.

## 7. Less Obvious Signals

The promotion of 'advanced technology' to the lead revenue descriptor, and the reframing of the modular business as a strategic capacity bet (targeting 4 million square feet), represents a real fixed-capital commitment to the thesis — and a future operating-deleverage risk if demand rolls. The fact that electrical revenue grew 88% on an organic basis versus 47% for mechanical signals a deliberate lean into the electrical-distribution and switchgear bottleneck. The unprompted 'labour is the cap' refrain tells you demand is real but not fully monetisable — a ceiling on the bull case.

## 8. Capital-Allocation Assessment

Operational trajectory: strengthening — a genuinely excellent, net-cash compounder executing flawlessly into its best-ever demand environment. But operational strength and valuation have decoupled: at 44 to 56 times earnings you are paying for peak-cycle margins on peak-cycle, single-end-market (56% data-centre) volume after a roughly fourfold run.

Competitive moat: scale cost, execution reputation, and a labour network in a fragmented, low-switching, re-bid trade — narrow, with a cyclically widening trend (the modular intellectual property, scale, and electrical-distribution depth are real structural gains, partly boosted by the cycle). Secular threat: none material — Comfort Systems is a beneficiary of electrification and the AI build-out, not a victim. The risk is cyclical (AI-capital-spending digestion), not terminal: the danger is paying a secular-growth multiple for a cyclical-peak earnings stream.

Management credibility 5 of 5; trajectory strengthening; moat narrow but real; moat trend widening; secular threat manageable.
skeptic_kill_fact: No single fact breaks the bear case. Every load-bearing figure in the bear thesis verifies against the first-quarter 2026 Form 8-K filing — revenue $2.87 billion up 56%, same-store up 51%, gross margin 26.3%, diluted earnings per share $10.51, order book $12.45 billion versus $6.89 billion a year earlier — and against earnings-call coverage confirming data centres at 56% of revenue, industrial at 75%, electrical up 88% on an organic basis, modular at 17% of revenue with a 4-million-square-foot target, and management naming labour as the binding constraint. The bear's core premise survives scrutiny: the stock is momentum-extended after a re-rating (price $1,967.41; 50-day moving average $1,831; 200-day moving average $1,253; 52-week range $490 to $2,074, roughly fourfold), the load-bearing catalyst has fired, and the AI-capital-spending re-rating is priced into a roughly 56.8 times trailing earnings multiple on peak-cycle margins in a single end market. There is no hidden disqualifier in the balance sheet, no dilution event, no meaningful debt maturity, no solvency risk.
skeptic_corrections:
Two factual corrections to the debate file, neither of which changes the overall conclusion.

First, the order-book year-over-year increase is 80.6% ($12.45 billion versus $6.89 billion), not the 74% figure cited in several places throughout the file. The 74% figure is internally inconsistent with the actual numbers it references.

Second, the net-cash figure in the bear thesis is understated in the bear's own favour. The Form 8-K shows total debt of approximately $39 million, not approximately $339 million, putting net cash at roughly $1.0 billion (approximately $29 per share) rather than the $711 million stated. The balance sheet is even stronger than the bear acknowledges. This slightly reinforces the 'quality without a balance-sheet valley' framing but does not help the bull case and does not alter the conclusion that the re-rating is already in the price.
skeptic_evidence: SEC Form 8-K (ex-99.1), Comfort Systems USA, filed 2026-04-23: Q1-2026 revenue $2.87B (+56%), diluted EPS $10.51, GM 26.3%, backlog $12.45B vs $6.89B (3/31/2025), cash ~$1.05B vs ~$39M debt, dividend raised to $0.80 (sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1035983/000110465926047679/)

Q1-2026 earnings call coverage, 2026-04-24: advanced technology/data centers = 56% of revenue, industrial 75%, electrical +88% organic / mechanical +47%, modular 17% of rev / 4M sq ft target end-2026; CFO 'it's always and forever for us, it's labor' / 'plenty more work we could take if we could possibly do it' (biggo.com/news/US_FIX_2026-04-24)

FMP live quote 2026-06-21: FIX $1,967.41, market cap $69.26B, 50d avg $1,830.81, 200d avg $1,253.42, 52wk $489.98–$2,073.99 — confirms ~4x run / momentum-extended melt-up and that the data-center re-rate is fully in the price
Implied multiple check: $1,967.41 / ~$34.65 TTM diluted EPS ≈ 56.8x trailing P/E — corroborates the bear's 'peak multiple on peak margins, no margin of safety vs ~$1,550 base SoP fair value' premise

scale.role: Mechanical/electrical contractor; data centers 56% of revenue, electrical +88% organic, backlog $12.45B (+80%).
scale.rationale: Very high-quality and the most AI-concentrated contractor (DC 56% of rev, backlog +80.6% to $12.45B, net cash ~$1.0B) — a strong LEVER, duplicative of EME within the contractor cluster. Skeptic verdict CONFIRMED_WITH_CORRECTIONS, corrections minor and balance-sheet-positive (backlog +80.6% not +74%; net cash even stronger than stated), so a ~8pt haircut off a ~60 franchise. The multiple prices peak margins as permanent (~57x trailing, base ~$1,550 is ~21% below spot) — that risk lives in valuation_posture; wait.
scale.demand_durability: High — DC 56% of revenue, the most capex-bolted contractor, but record margins are the risk.
